title: Decision: Stage2 authority migrated to the semantic-object emitter; Stage2.5 retired from the active mainline
decision: Stage2 authority migrated from the legacy wide-row extractor to the true paper-driven semantic-object emitter. Semantic objects are now the authoritative Stage2 intermediate representation, and the deterministic compatibility adapter is the required bridge into the legacy wide-row surface used by unchanged Stage3 and Stage5 consumers. Stage2.5 is retired from the active pipeline. The legacy auto_extract_weak_labels_v7pilot_r3_fixparse.py path is deprecated and retained only for fallback or debug use. The adapter is a bridge, not a semantic inference layer, and known hidden Stage3 dependencies on legacy row semantics remain unresolved in this change.
reason: Active governance documents now define semantic Stage2 as the authoritative extraction boundary and the compatibility adapter as the only sanctioned migration bridge. The governed decision memory surface was stale after rebuild because this architecture transition was not yet represented in dec.tsv.
impact: Decision memory now matches the active architecture: semantic Stage2 -> compatibility adapter -> Stage3 -> Stage5. Queries against governed memory can recover the current architecture state without relying on stale legacy wide-row Stage2 assumptions.

title: Decision: Blank or unloaded formulations must preserve null drug fields through workbook-generation and representation-repair
decision: Blank or unloaded formulations must have a null drug field. Do not inherit drug identity from paper-level mentions, family-level loaded siblings, or historical workbook labels. Do not assign a drug by substring-based matching such as `detect_drug_name(...)` unless formulation-level evidence explicitly supports that row. Representation-repair must respect and preserve null drug fields rather than synthesizing a drug value from full-text fallback.
reason: The 2026-03-28 blank/unloaded drug contamination audit confirmed that benchmark-valid Stage5 and current v7 value-alignment artifacts kept these rows null, while the downstream representation-repair workbook lineage introduced wrong Etoposide, FITC, XAN, and upstream GAR values by substring fallback and older workbook-lineage contamination.
impact: This rule prevents recurrence in reviewer surfaces: blank/unloaded rows remain null for drug identity unless row-local evidence exists, and downstream workbook-refresh logic can no longer override null drug fields from paper-level or substring-only signals.

title: Decision: Layer 3 cross-audit is a governed post-annotation reviewer-risk surface
decision: Layer 3 cross-audit is a report-only post-annotation reviewer-risk surface for the compact value workbook. It combines a deterministic rule audit with optional Gemini and NVIDIA auditor task outputs into one cell-level risk report, but it does not edit workbook cells or alter benchmark-valid artifacts.
reason: The workbook audit task required a reusable, governed way to surface suspicious cells without conflating reviewer signals with GT truth. Existing Layer 3 helpers covered workbook construction and evidence handoff, but not a consolidated one-row-per-flagged-cell audit layer.
impact: Future Layer 3 review can reuse one governed cross-audit workflow instead of ad hoc paper-scoped audits. Gemini and NVIDIA outputs remain optional supporting signals, and the merged report remains a human-review surface rather than a data-repair mechanism.

title: Decision: Layer 3 reviewer surfaces retain only explicit source-supported values
decision: Layer 3 reviewer surfaces retain only explicit source-supported values. Computed, normalized, inferred, inherited, or direction-inverted values do not count as direct support, and model agreement does not upgrade them into GT. The existing blank/unloaded null-drug rule remains in force.
reason: The new cross-audit framework needs a governed evidence rule that matches the Layer 3 GT principle: support must come from explicit source text or tables for the current paper, not from derivation or reviewer convenience.
impact: This rule allows reviewer surfaces to flag derived or ambiguous cells consistently across papers, keeps model-based auditors in an audit-only role, and preserves the blank/unloaded null-drug safeguard as a standing Layer 3 constraint.

title: Decision: Layer 3 cross-audit live model execution must be bounded and observable
decision: When live Gemini or NVIDIA Layer 3 auditor execution is used, run the refined rule audit first and then execute only a bounded candidate slice with explicit skip flags, request caps, timeouts, retry limits, progress logging, and optional per-batch partial writes. The workflow may be reduced to rule-only mode with --rules-only when model execution is not required.
reason: The interrupted 2026-03-28 run showed that sequential per-cell live model execution without explicit runtime controls is operationally unsafe for a reviewer-facing audit workflow even when the backend itself is healthy.
impact: Future Layer 3 review and debugging can use short smoke tests, preserve partial backend outputs, and distinguish backend blocking from ordinary wall-clock latency without treating noisy library stderr as the root problem.

title: Decision: Layer 3 reviewer-facing outputs form a formulation-centered audit and governance layer
decision: Layer 3 reviewer-facing outputs are not only evaluation helpers. They are also part of the governed production audit and governance layer around the frozen formulation database. The preferred reviewer entry object is one formulation row, with two linked human-review layers: formulation existence and identity audit first, then value credibility audit. Value credibility depends on structure correctness, many apparent value errors are projections of identity errors, and the current repo state remains partially present but not unified. The benchmark-valid endpoint remains final_formulation_table_v1.tsv, and reviewer-facing audit outputs remain downstream support surfaces that must not mutate benchmark-valid outputs.
reason: Architecture review showed that the repo already has governed paper-risk, formulation-audit, field-review, cell-risk, and evidence-handoff surfaces, but they are fragmented and were too often described as evaluation-only helpers. The newer design conclusion needs one explicit governed decision so future docs and memory queries recover the formulation-centered audit interpretation directly.
impact: Future Layer 3 design work can stay formulation-centered, define functional units such as Formulation Index Builder, Structure Review Builder, Value Risk Builder, and Evidence Handoff Builder, and keep the benchmark-valid database endpoint unchanged while treating reviewer-facing audit outputs as a production governance surface.
